title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about climate change solutions. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Beyond the Headlines
content.subtitle: An article about the range of approaches to addressing climate change.
content.text:
The public conversation about climate change has a peculiar quality. It oscillates between two extremes: catastrophism, which holds that the situation is so dire that normal life is a form of denial, and techno-optimism, which insists that innovation will solve everything before things get truly bad. [31] Both framings share a common flaw: they position ordinary people as spectators in a drama whose outcome will be determined elsewhere — by governments, by corporations, or by engineers not yet born. [/31] Neither position encourages the kind of engaged, practical thinking that the situation actually requires.

The science of climate change is settled in its broad conclusions: temperatures are rising due to human activity, the consequences are already visible and will worsen, and action taken now is vastly more effective than action taken later. What is less settled — and considerably more interesting — is the question of which interventions are most effective. [32] Researchers have found that different solutions vary enormously in their impact. Reducing food waste, for instance, has a greater potential to reduce emissions than switching to electric vehicles, a fact that surprises most people when they first encounter it. [/32]

The most sophisticated analyses of climate solutions — including Project Drawdown, a major international research effort — have found that the most impactful measures tend to cluster around three areas: energy production, land use, and the empowerment of women and girls through education. [33] This last category surprises many readers, but the data is consistent: societies in which women have full educational and economic opportunities have lower birth rates and lower per-capita emissions than those where such opportunities are absent. [/33] The climate and gender equality are, it turns out, deeply connected issues.

For individuals navigating their own contribution, the landscape is complicated by significant misinformation. The concept of the personal carbon footprint was popularised in the 2000s by a BP advertising campaign — a fact that illustrates how effectively the fossil fuel industry shifted moral responsibility from producers to consumers. [34] This does not mean personal choices are irrelevant, but it does mean that campaigns focused primarily on individual lifestyle changes rather than systemic action are addressing a symptom rather than the cause. [/34]

At the same time, dismissing personal action entirely is both intellectually dishonest and strategically counterproductive. [35] The same people who make significant changes to their own consumption tend to become more vocal advocates for policy change — the personal and political are not opposites but reinforcing forces. [/35] Behavioural researchers call this consistency theory: humans prefer to act in ways that align with their stated values, and those who act tend to advocate.

The picture that emerges from serious engagement with climate science is neither hopeless nor comfortable. The tools and technologies needed to limit warming to manageable levels largely exist. What is lacking is not innovation but will — political, institutional, and cultural. [36] The transition required is, in historical terms, entirely achievable; transformations of comparable scale have happened before, driven by far smaller incentives. The question is whether the pace will match the need. [/36]

questions.question: The writer criticises both catastrophism and techno-optimism because they
options: A) are based on inaccurate scientific data.
B) make ordinary people feel they have no meaningful role in addressing climate change.
C) are promoted primarily by people with financial interests in the issue.
D) lead to unrealistic expectations about what governments can achieve.
questions.explanation: Both framings 'position ordinary people as spectators' — passive observers rather than active participants — which is the flaw the writer identifies.

questions.question: The writer mentions the comparison between food waste and electric vehicles in order to show that
options: A) electric vehicles are not an effective solution to climate change.
B) the most impactful climate solutions are often counterintuitive.
C) people should focus on food systems rather than transport.
D) most people underestimate the importance of dietary choices.
questions.explanation: The comparison is described as 'a fact that surprises most people', showing that the most impactful solutions can be unexpected and counterintuitive.

questions.question: The writer includes women's education and empowerment as a climate solution to demonstrate that
options: A) climate change disproportionately affects women in developing countries.
B) gender inequality is a more urgent issue than climate change.
C) climate change and social justice issues are unexpectedly interconnected.
D) international research projects tend to focus too much on social issues.
questions.explanation: The writer says 'The climate and gender equality are, it turns out, deeply connected issues', and notes the finding 'surprises many readers', illustrating an unexpected interconnection.

questions.question: The writer's point about BP's carbon footprint campaign is that
options: A) personal lifestyle choices have no real effect on carbon emissions.
B) the fossil fuel industry successfully redirected blame for emissions onto consumers.
C) advertising campaigns are an effective way to promote environmental awareness.
D) the concept of the carbon footprint is scientifically inaccurate.
questions.explanation: The writer says the concept 'illustrates how effectively the fossil fuel industry shifted moral responsibility from producers to consumers'.

questions.question: According to the writer, personal action and political advocacy are
options: A) in conflict, as one distracts from the other.
B) both ineffective compared to institutional change.
C) mutually reinforcing rather than separate activities.
D) only effective when organised through campaigns.
questions.explanation: The writer says 'the personal and political are not opposites but reinforcing forces', and behavioural research supports this through consistency theory.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the writer argues that addressing climate change is
options: A) impossible without technological breakthroughs that do not yet exist.
B) primarily a question of political and cultural determination rather than capability.
C) achievable but will inevitably happen too slowly to prevent serious damage.
D) similar in scale to challenges that previous generations found impossible.
questions.explanation: The writer says 'The tools and technologies needed...largely exist. What is lacking is not innovation but will — political, institutional, and cultural', making it a question of determination.
